{"parentUuid":"c225c079-dc35-448f-929b-50878d2408bc","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"ac699526b8bf7af7d","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01Hx9pbssGL7mb4JSsPRqnCn","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"combine modern country boundaries approximate historical colonial territories GIS methodology union dissolve polygons\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"GitHub - aourednik/historical-basemaps: Collection of georeferenced boundaries of world countries and cultural regions for use in mapping historical data on global or continental scale · GitHub\",\"url\":\"https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset - Guy Schvitz, Luc Girardin, Seraina Rüegger, Nils B. Weidmann, Lars-Erik Cederman, Kristian Skrede Gleditsch, 2022\",\"url\":\"https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/00220027211013563\"},{\"title\":\"Dissolve boundaries | Documentation\",\"url\":\"https://learn.arcgis.com/en/projects/dissolve-boundaries/\"},{\"title\":\"[Feature] Dissolve Boundaries · Issue #2422 · r-spatial/sf\",\"url\":\"https://github.com/r-spatial/sf/issues/2422\"},{\"title\":\"(De)Colonial historical geography and historical GIS - ScienceDirect\",\"url\":\"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305748822000846\"},{\"title\":\"Where to Find GIS Data for Historical Country Boundaries - Geography Realm\",\"url\":\"https://www.geographyrealm.com/find-gis-data-historical-country-boundaries/\"},{\"title\":\"About the Project | Atlas of Historical County Boundaries Project\",\"url\":\"https://publications.newberry.org/ahcb/project.html\"},{\"title\":\"Dissolve Boundaries (Standard Feature Analysis)—ArcGIS Pro | Documentation\",\"url\":\"https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/tool-reference/feature-analysis/dissolve-boundaries.htm\"},{\"title\":\"geoBoundaries\",\"url\":\"https://www.geoboundaries.org/\"},{\"title\":\"Political Geography – Introduction to Human Geography\",\"url\":\"https://pressbooks.pub/humangeo/chapter/political-geography/\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, here's what I found relevant to your query about combining modern country boundaries to approximate historical colonial territories using GIS methodology:\n\n## Key GIS Datasets and Resources\n\nCShapes 2.0 is a GIS dataset that maps the borders of states and dependent territories from 1886 through 2019, and it maps the borders of colonies and other dependencies, providing near complete global coverage of political units throughout recent history.\n\nThe historical-basemaps project includes a SUBJECTO field for the name of the colonial power exercising authority, and a BORDERPRECISION field with ordinal values 1 (approximate), 2 (moderately precise) and 3 (determined by international law).\n\n## GIS Methodology: Dissolve/Union Operations\n\nThe Dissolve Boundaries operation finds polygons that overlap or share a common boundary and merges them together to form a single polygon, with the ability to control which boundaries are merged by specifying a field. For example, if you have a layer of counties with a State_Name field, adjacent counties can be merged together if they have the same value for State_Name, with the end result being a layer of state boundaries.\n\n## Handling Approximate and Overlapping Colonial Boundaries\n\nWhen rendering, transparent layers and blur effects provide a convenient way to deal with fuzzy and overlapping borders of pre-modern societies, and visual distinction between precise and approximate borders can be achieved on the same map.\n\nThe HGIS de las Indias site features thousands of boundary files covering colonial Spanish America from 1701 – 1808.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"41051448-c7fc-44fd-9268-7fe84dde5241","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:12:23.737Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"c225c079-dc35-448f-929b-50878d2408bc","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"41051448-c7fc-44fd-9268-7fe84dde5241","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"ac699526b8bf7af7d","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_01VEHq1xMXUKeRTDLmJsEpSr","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Now I have enough information gathered. Let me compile the comprehensive answer.\n\n---\n\n# Comprehensive Research Report: Polygon Data for Small Territories, Historical Colonial Territories, and Pre-1886 European States\n\n## GROUP 1: Modern Small Territories\n\n### Can GADM and Natural Earth provide polygons for ALL of these?\n\n**Short answer: Almost all. GADM covers ~95% of your list, Natural Earth covers ~90%. Between the two plus OpenStreetMap, you can get 100% coverage.**\n\n### GADM (v4.1) Coverage\n\n**URL:** https://gadm.org/download_country.html\n**Format:** GeoPackage, Shapefile, GeoJSON, KMZ\n**Free:** Yes, for academic/non-commercial use\n**License:** Non-commercial; redistribution not allowed without permission\n\nGADM explicitly lists all of the following in its dropdown for individual download:\n\n| Territory | GADM? | Notes |\n|-----------|-------|-------|\n| Andorra | YES | |\n| Liechtenstein | YES | |\n| Monaco | YES | |\n| San Marino | YES | |\n| Vatican City | YES | |\n| Gibraltar | YES | |\n| Ceuta/Melilla | **NO** (not standalone) | Available as admin-level subdivisions under **Spain** (GADM level 1) |\n| Bermuda | YES | |\n| Cayman Islands | YES | |\n| British Virgin Islands | YES | |\n| Montserrat | YES | |\n| Anguilla | YES | |\n| Turks and Caicos | YES | |\n| Guam | YES | |\n| American Samoa | YES | |\n| Northern Mariana Islands | YES | |\n| Wake Island | **NO** (not standalone) | Under **United States Minor Outlying Islands** (GADM code UMI), with Wake as a level-1 subdivision polygon |\n| Midway | **NO** (not standalone) | Under **United States Minor Outlying Islands** (GADM code UMI), with Midway as a level-1 subdivision polygon |\n| Aruba | YES | |\n| Curacao | YES | |\n| Sint Maarten | YES | |\n| Bonaire/Sint Eustatius/Saba | YES | Listed as one entry |\n| Cook Islands | YES | |\n| Niue | YES | |\n| Tokelau | YES | |\n| Pitcairn Islands | YES | |\n| Wallis and Futuna | YES | |\n| Saint-Barthelemy | YES | |\n| Saint-Martin (French) | YES | |\n| Saint Pierre and Miquelon | YES | |\n| Aland Islands | YES | |\n| Guernsey | YES | |\n| Jersey | YES | |\n| Isle of Man | YES | |\n| Svalbard and Jan Mayen | YES | |\n| Christmas Island | YES | |\n| Cocos (Keeling) Islands | YES | |\n| Norfolk Island | YES | |\n| Heard Island and McDonald Islands | YES | |\n| French Southern Territories | YES | |\n| Bouvet Island | YES | |\n| Mayotte | YES | |\n| Reunion | YES | |\n| Martinique | YES | |\n| Guadeloupe | YES | |\n| French Guiana | YES | |\n| French Polynesia | YES | |\n| New Caledonia | YES | |\n\n**Key finding:** Wake Island and Midway are NOT standalone entries in GADM but are available as level-1 subdivisions within \"United States Minor Outlying Islands\" (9 individual island polygons: Midway, Wake, Navassa, Johnston, Kingman Reef, Palmyra, Howland, Baker, Jarvis). Ceuta and Melilla are available as admin-level subdivisions of Spain.\n\n### Natural Earth (10m) Coverage\n\n**URL:** https://www.naturalearthdata.com/downloads/10m-cultural-vectors/\n**Format:** Shapefile (also GeoJSON via GitHub mirror at https://github.com/martynafford/natural-earth-geojson)\n**Free:** Yes, public domain\n**License:** Public domain, no restrictions\n\nNatural Earth's 10m Admin 0 dataset includes 258 countries and 298 map units, 360 subunits. The Admin 0 Details layer was confirmed to include:\n- Vatican (Holy See), Monaco, Gibraltar, Bermuda, Svalbard, Bouvet Island\n- Wake Atoll and Midway Islands (as US Minor Outlying Islands)\n- All the European microstates, Caribbean territories, Pacific territories\n\n**Important caveat:** At 1:50m and 1:110m scales, many tiny territories are either omitted or represented as points rather than polygons. You MUST use the **1:10m scale** for complete coverage of microstates and small islands.\n\nNatural Earth also has a dedicated **\"Minor Islands\"** layer (https://www.naturalearthdata.com/downloads/10m-physical-vectors/10m-minor-islands/) that provides additional small ocean island polygons not in the main admin layers.\n\n### OpenStreetMap (Fallback)\n\n**URL:** https://polygons.openstreetmap.fr/ or Nominatim API with `polygon_geojson=1`\n**Format:** GeoJSON, WKT, SVG\n**Free:** Yes, ODbL license\n\nFor any territory missing from GADM or Natural Earth, OpenStreetMap provides polygon extraction using OSM relation IDs. This is particularly useful for Wake Island and Midway if you need standalone polygons.\n\n### Recommendation for Group 1\n\nUse **GADM 4.1** as the primary source (best administrative detail, sub-national levels). Supplement with **Natural Earth 10m** for standardized attributes and map-ready data. For Wake Island and Midway specifically, download GADM's \"United States Minor Outlying Islands\" at admin level 1 to get individual island polygons. For Ceuta and Melilla, download GADM's Spain at admin level 1.\n\n---\n\n## GROUP 2: Historical Colonial Territories\n\n### CShapes 2.0 -- The Primary Source\n\n**URL:** https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/\n**Format:** CSV, GeoJSON, Shapefile, SQL, R package (`cshapes`)\n**Free:** Yes (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)\n**Coverage:** 1886-2019 for global; 1816-2019 for Europe\n\nCShapes 2.0 maps borders of both independent states AND dependent territories (colonies, protectorates, mandates, occupied territories) from 1886 onward. It records exact dates of territorial changes. This is the best source for your historical colonial territories.\n\n**Critical note for your project:** Your existing `cshapes.csv` file (316 lines) contains only independent states extracted from CShapes. The full CShapes 2.0 dataset includes colonial dependencies as well -- you would need to re-extract it to include dependent territories.\n\n### Territory-by-territory analysis:\n\n| Colonial Territory | Period | In CShapes 2.0? | Can modern borders approximate? | Accuracy of modern-border approach |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **British Malaya** | 1922-1957 | YES (from 1886) | Peninsular Malaysia + Singapore | MODERATE -- excludes Borneo territories (Sarawak, North Borneo were separate); internal borders between Federated/Unfederated Malay States differ |\n| **French Indochina** | 1887-1954 | YES (from 1887) | Vietnam + Laos + Cambodia | GOOD -- but Guangzhouwan (leased Chinese territory, 1898-1945) is missing; modern Vietnam/Laos/Cambodia combined is a reasonable approximation |\n| **Belgian Congo** | 1885-1960 | YES (from 1886) | Modern DRC | GOOD -- borders are essentially identical; minor differences with Ruanda-Urundi (modern Rwanda/Burundi) which was a separate mandate |\n| **German East Africa** | 1884-1919 | YES (from 1886) | Modern Tanzania | MODERATE -- German East Africa included modern Tanzania + Rwanda + Burundi; using Tanzania alone misses ~30% of the territory |\n| **British East Africa** | 1895-1963 | YES (from 1895) | Kenya + Uganda | MODERATE -- Uganda Protectorate and East Africa Protectorate were administratively separate; modern Kenya + Uganda is close but boundary details differ, and Zanzibar was separate |\n| **French West Africa (AOF)** | 1895-1960 | YES (from 1895) | 8 modern countries: Mauritania, Senegal, Mali, Guinea, Ivory Coast, Burkina Faso, Niger, Benin | GOOD -- st_union() of these 8 countries is a very reasonable approximation; internal colonial borders evolved over time |\n| **French Equatorial Africa (AEF)** | 1910-1958 | YES (from 1910) | 4 modern countries: Chad, Central African Republic, Republic of Congo, Gabon | GOOD -- st_union() of these 4 is close; Cameroon was separate (German until 1919, then split French/British mandate) |\n\n### Can modern country boundaries be COMBINED to approximate historical colonial territories?\n\n**YES, with caveats.** The `sf::st_union()` approach (dissolving modern country polygons into one) works well for:\n- French Indochina (Vietnam + Laos + Cambodia)\n- Belgian Congo (DRC alone)\n- French West Africa (8-country union)\n- French Equatorial Africa (4-country union)\n\nIt works with **important caveats** for:\n- German East Africa: Must include Tanzania + Rwanda + Burundi (not just Tanzania)\n- British East Africa: Must define carefully -- Kenya + Uganda + potentially parts of Zanzibar\n- British Malaya: Peninsular Malaysia only (not Borneo); Singapore was part of Straits Settlements\n\n**However, CShapes 2.0 already has the actual colonial boundaries**, so you should prefer those over the modern-border approximation approach. The modern-border union is a fallback if CShapes data proves difficult to work with for a specific territory.\n\n---\n\n## GROUP 3: Pre-1886 European States (Italian Pre-Unification States)\n\nThis is the hardest group. CShapes 2.0's global coverage starts at 1886, well after Italian unification (1860-1870). However, several sources exist:\n\n### Source 1: CShapes-Europe (1816-2019) -- BEST OPTION\n\n**URL:** https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/\n**Format:** Shapefile, GeoJSON, CSV, R package\n**Free:** Yes (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)\n**Coverage:** Europe from 1816\n\nCShapes-Europe extends the dataset back to 1816 specifically for European states, and the documentation explicitly states it includes \"dozens of microstates that existed before the German and Italian unifications.\" This means it should cover:\n\n| State | Period | Likely in CShapes-Europe? |\n|---|---|---|\n| Papal States | pre-1870 | YES (1816-1870) |\n| Kingdom of Two Sicilies | pre-1860 | YES (1816-1860) |\n| Duchy of Modena | pre-1860 | YES (1816-1860) |\n| Duchy of Parma | pre-1860 | YES (1816-1860) |\n| Grand Duchy of Tuscany | pre-1860 | YES (1816-1860) |\n| Kingdom of Sardinia | pre-1860 | YES (1816-1861) |\n| Ionian Islands (British) | pre-1864 | LIKELY (1816-1864) |\n\n**This is your best bet and should be the first source you try.**\n\n### Source 2: Geo-Larhra -- Italy 1815-1866\n\n**URL:** http://geo-larhra.ish-lyon.cnrs.fr/?q=geocatalogue/vectors\n**Format:** Shapefile\n**Free:** Likely yes (academic project from University of Lyon/CNRS)\n**Coverage:** International boundaries in Italy, 1815-1866\n\nThis dataset specifically covers Italian state boundaries during the pre-unification period. It should include all the Italian states you listed. The site was slow to load during testing, but the data is referenced by multiple academic GIS resource guides.\n\n### Source 3: Euratlas Periodis\n\n**URL:** https://www.euratlas.net/shop/maps_gis/gis_1800.html\n**Format:** Shapefile\n**Free:** NO -- 150 EUR per century (simple license)\n**Coverage:** Europe, century-by-century (year 1 to 2000)\n\nEuratlas provides georeferenced historical vector data at centennial intervals. The 1800 dataset confirms it includes Italian states: Naples, Parma, Tuscany, Sardinia, San Marino, and Papal States. **However, it only has data at 100-year intervals (1800, 1900) -- there is no 1850 or 1860 snapshot**, which limits its usefulness for the Italian unification period specifically. Additionally, it costs 150 EUR per dataset.\n\n### Source 4: Centennia Historical Atlas (KML)\n\n**URL:** http://historicalatlas.com/CRE/\n**Format:** KML\n**Free:** NO -- extremely expensive ($75 for single year, $3,125 for 1789-1939 period, $12,500 for complete 1000-2003)\n**Coverage:** Europe, 1000 AD to 2003, very detailed temporal resolution\n\nThe Centennia dataset covers the Italian unification period in detail and includes daily-level boundary changes. However, the price makes it impractical for most academic projects.\n\n### Source 5: Historical Basemaps (aourednik) -- FREE but Limited\n\n**URL:** https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps\n**Format:** GeoJSON\n**Free:** Yes\n**Coverage:** Various years from 2000 BC to 2010\n\n**Critical limitation:** Available years jump from **1815 directly to 1880**, with no data for 1850 or 1860. This means the Italian unification period falls squarely in the gap. The 1815 file would show the post-Congress of Vienna arrangement, which is close to but not identical to the 1850s situation. Also, spatial accuracy is +/- 40 miles (student-created data from ThinkQuest).\n\n### Source 6: World Historical GIS Data (idris-maps/ThinkQuest archive)\n\n**URL:** https://github.com/idris-maps/world-historical-gis-data\n**Format:** TopoJSON\n**Free:** Yes\n**Coverage:** 2000 BC to 1994 (specific snapshots only: 1783, 1880, 1914, 1920...)\n\nSame underlying data as historical-basemaps but in TopoJSON format. **No 1850 or 1860 snapshots available.** Spatial error of roughly +/- 40 miles.\n\n### Recommendation for Group 3\n\n1. **First choice: CShapes-Europe (1816-2019)** -- Download the full CShapes 2.0 dataset and filter for the European extension. It should include all pre-unification Italian states and the Ionian Islands from 1816 onward. Free, academic quality, maintained by ETH Zurich.\n\n2. **Second choice: Geo-Larhra Italy 1815-1866** -- Purpose-built for exactly your use case. Free academic resource from CNRS/Lyon, specifically covering Italian state boundaries in the pre-unification period.\n\n3. **Third choice (paid): Euratlas 1800** -- High-quality academic shapefile but only at the 1800 snapshot, not 1850. 150 EUR.\n\n4. **Fallback: Approximate from modern Italian regions** -- Modern Italian administrative regions (GADM admin level 1 or 2) can be combined to roughly approximate pre-unification states. For example, the Kingdom of Two Sicilies corresponds roughly to modern regions of Sicily, Calabria, Basilicata, Puglia, Campania, Molise, and Abruzzi. The Papal States correspond roughly to Lazio, Umbria, Marche, and parts of Emilia-Romagna. This is less accurate but free and immediately available.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| Group | Best Source | URL | Format | Free? | Accuracy |\n|-------|-----------|-----|--------|-------|----------|\n| Modern small territories | GADM 4.1 | https://gadm.org/download_country.html | Shapefile/GeoJSON/GeoPackage | Yes (non-commercial) | High |\n| Modern small territories (supplement) | Natural Earth 10m | https://www.naturalearthdata.com/downloads/10m-cultural-vectors/ | Shapefile | Yes (public domain) | Good (cartographic) |\n| Wake/Midway | GADM UMI level-1 | https://gadm.org/maps/UMI_1.html | Shapefile/GeoJSON | Yes | High |\n| Ceuta/Melilla | GADM Spain level-1 | https://gadm.org/download_country.html (Spain) | Shapefile/GeoJSON | Yes | High |\n| Colonial territories (1886+) | CShapes 2.0 | https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/ | Shapefile/GeoJSON/CSV/R pkg | Yes (CC BY-NC-SA) | High (exact dates) |\n| Colonial approximation | Modern borders + st_union() | GADM or Natural Earth | Any | Yes | Moderate (see caveats) |\n| Pre-1886 Italian states | CShapes-Europe (1816+) | https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/ | Shapefile/GeoJSON/CSV | Yes (CC BY-NC-SA) | High |\n| Pre-1866 Italian states | Geo-Larhra | http://geo-larhra.ish-lyon.cnrs.fr/ | Shapefile | Yes (academic) | Good |\n| Pre-1886 Italian states (paid) | Euratlas | https://www.euratlas.net/shop/maps_gis/ | Shapefile | No (150 EUR/century) | High |\n| Historical basemaps (free fallback) | aourednik/historical-basemaps | https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps | GeoJSON | Yes | Low (+/- 40 miles) |\n\nSources:\n- [GADM](https://gadm.org/)\n- [GADM Country Download](https://gadm.org/download_country.html)\n- [GADM US Minor Outlying Islands Level 1](https://gadm.org/maps/UMI_1.html)\n- [Natural Earth Admin 0
